stem: In the ring \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \) with \( [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \), how many nonzero elements are zero divisors? (A nonzero \( x \) is a zero divisor if \( x y \equiv 0 \pmod{[[nval]]} \) for some nonzero \( y \).)
solution: The nonzero non-units of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \) are exactly its zero divisors. There are \( [[nval]] \) residues, of which \( \varphi([[nval]]) = [[p]]^{[[a]]-1}([[p]]-1)\cdot[[q]]^{[[b]]-1}([[q]]-1) \) are units and one is \( 0 \). So the number of nonzero zero divisors is \( [[nval]] - \varphi([[nval]]) - 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( n - \varphi(n) \), the count of all non-units; it still includes \( 0 \), which is not counted as a (nonzero) zero divisor.
This counts the units \( \varphi(n) \) instead of the zero divisors.
This counts every nonzero residue, treating \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \) as if it had no zero divisors beyond exclusion of \( 0 \).
This subtracts one from the unit count \( \varphi(n) \) rather than computing \( n - \varphi(n) - 1 \).
This is two fewer than the correct count.
This is three more than the correct count.

stem: In the ring \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \), the element \( x = [[x]] \) is a zero divisor. What is the SMALLEST positive integer \( y \) such that \( x y \equiv 0 \pmod{[[n]]} \)?
solution: \( xy \equiv 0 \pmod{[[n]]} \) means \( n \mid xy \). Writing \( d = \gcd([[x]],[[n]]) \), this reduces to \( \frac{n}{d} \mid y \cdot \frac{x}{d} \), and since \( \frac{x}{d} \) and \( \frac{n}{d} \) are coprime, the smallest positive \( y \) is \( \frac{n}{d} = \dfrac{[[n]]}{\gcd([[x]],[[n]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \gcd(x,n) \) itself, the FACTOR that \( n \) shares with \( x \), not the smallest annihilating multiplier \( n/\gcd(x,n) \).
This is the additive complement of \( x \), unrelated to the multiplicative annihilator condition \( xy \equiv 0 \).
This just repeats \( x \) itself, which does not generally satisfy \( x\cdot x \equiv 0 \pmod{n} \).

This is \( y = n \), which does satisfy \( xy\equiv 0 \) but is not the SMALLEST such positive \( y \) (that representative is \( 0 \), excluded, and the smallest positive one is smaller than \( n \) itself).
This is one more than the correct smallest \( y \).
This is one fewer than the correct smallest \( y \).

stem: How many nonzero elements of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \), where \( [[p]] \) is prime, are zero divisors?
solution: \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \) is a field when \( [[p]] \) is prime (every nonzero element is a unit, by Bezout / Fermat), and a field has NO zero divisors. So the count is \( [[answer]] = 0 \).
distractor_rationales: This treats every nonzero element as a zero divisor; but \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \) is a field, so every nonzero element is a UNIT, not a zero divisor.
This is one less than \( p-1 \), still wrongly assuming most nonzero elements are zero divisors.
This assumes exactly one nonzero zero divisor exists; a field has none at all.
This assumes exactly two nonzero zero divisors exist; a field has none at all.
This assumes exactly three nonzero zero divisors exist; a field has none at all.
This is the modulus \( [[p]] \) itself, unrelated to counting zero divisors.

stem: In the ring \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \), the element \( x = [[x]] \) is a zero divisor. How many NONZERO elements \( y \in \{1, \dots, [[n]]-1\} \) satisfy \( x y \equiv 0 \pmod{[[n]]} \)?
solution: The set of \( y \in \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) with \( xy\equiv 0 \pmod{[[n]]} \) (the annihilator of \( x \)) has exactly \( d = \gcd([[x]],[[n]]) \) elements, namely the multiples of \( n/d \), including \( y=0 \). Excluding \( y=0 \) leaves \( d - 1 = \gcd([[x]],[[n]]) - 1 = [[answer]] \) nonzero solutions.
distractor_rationales: This includes \( y=0 \) in the count; the question asks only for NONZERO solutions \( y \).
This is the smallest positive annihilating \( y \) (namely \( n/\gcd(x,n) \)), not the total NUMBER of nonzero \( y \) that work.
This is one less than the smallest positive annihilating \( y \), not the count of all such \( y \).
This is the additive complement of \( x \), unrelated to counting annihilator solutions.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is two more than the correct count.
